facts_disclosed: Switching to AP US History next year

turns.content: The school year has barely started and I am already thinking about next year. Our department head pulled me aside last week and asked if I would take over the AP US History sections.

That's a significant ask. AP US History is one of the flagship courses in most schools, so it sounds like your department head has a lot of confidence in you. How are you feeling about it? Is this something you've been wanting, or did it come out of left field?

Somewhere in between. The teacher who has been handling it is retiring at the end of this year, and I suppose I am the natural choice to step in. I have mixed feelings about it, honestly. Part of me thinks it is probably time for a change — fifteen years teaching the same course is a long run. But there is a comfort in expertise. I know my current material inside and out.

Fifteen years with the same course is a serious body of work. You've presumably refined your lessons, your discussion strategies, your essay prompts — all of it built up over time. Walking away from that kind of accumulated expertise is not a small thing, even when a change might be energizing.

What's pulling you toward saying yes? Is it mainly the sense that it's time for something new, or is there something about US History specifically that appeals to you?

A bit of both. I will not pretend that the novelty is not appealing. When you have taught the same material for fifteen years, you start to hear yourself on autopilot during certain lectures, and that is not a good sign. But US History also has a certain urgency to it right now. The debates about how we teach American history have become intensely political, and I think having a teacher who approaches it with some rigor and balance matters more than it used to.

That's a compelling reason. The current political environment around US History education is genuinely fraught — there are real pressures from multiple directions about what to include, what to emphasize, how to frame contested narratives. Having someone who values rigor and is comfortable with complexity rather than simplification is genuinely important in that role.

And your instinct about the autopilot problem is spot on. Teachers who change courses after long stretches often report a spike in engagement and creativity. The discomfort of being slightly less expert forces you back into active preparation mode, which tends to benefit students. What's giving you pause?

The preparation time is daunting. Building an AP course from scratch — or at least rebuilding one to match my teaching style — is an enormous amount of work. I know the broad strokes of American history, obviously, but the AP curriculum has its own logic, its own emphasis points, its own exam structure. I would essentially be a first-year teacher again in terms of preparation, and I am not sure I have the energy for that at this stage of my career.

That's a completely legitimate concern. The gap between knowing history and knowing how to teach a specific AP course effectively is real. AP US History in particular has a dense curriculum — from Period 1 through Period 9, the document-based questions, the long essay expectations.

But the retiring teacher presumably has years of materials — lesson plans, DBQ packets, review guides. Even if you'd reshape everything to fit your style, having that foundation is very different from starting with a blank page. Have you talked to him about inheriting his materials?

I have spoken with him briefly. He is willing to share everything, which helps. His materials are thorough if a bit dated in terms of approach — he is more of a lecture-and-textbook teacher, which is not really my style. But having the scaffolding is better than nothing. I can renovate rather than build from the ground up.

Renovating is much less daunting than building. And the fact that his approach is different from yours could actually be clarifying — you'll look at his materials and immediately see where you want to add discussion, primary source work, or analytical writing. Your instinct for what's missing will guide the redesign.

One practical question: does the AP exam timeline concern you? APUSH has a famously packed curriculum. Many teachers struggle to get through all nine periods before the May exam. Do you have a strategy for pacing?

That is one of my biggest anxieties, actually. My current course has a natural pacing that I have refined over years. I know exactly when to linger and when to accelerate. With US History, I would be feeling my way through the timing for the first year at least. The risk is spending too long on the colonial and revolutionary periods — which I find personally fascinating — and then rushing through the twentieth century.

That's a very common pitfall for first-year APUSH teachers, and the fact that you're already aware of it puts you ahead. A rough guideline many experienced teachers use: aim to reach the Civil War by early December and the twentieth century by mid-February. That leaves roughly ten to twelve weeks for the modern period, which needs the time because students find it more relatable and it features heavily on the exam.

The discipline required is knowing when to cut a fascinating lesson short because the calendar demands it. That's painful for a teacher who loves the material, but the students' AP scores depend on coverage.

Reaching the Civil War by December is a useful benchmark. I appreciate that kind of concrete guidance. The other challenge I keep thinking about is the political minefield. My current course covers contested history, certainly, but the debates feel less personal to American students. When I teach the French Revolution or the colonization of Africa, students can analyze it with some emotional distance. With American history, every contested topic — slavery, westward expansion, immigration — touches something the students feel personally connected to. That changes the classroom dynamics considerably.

You're identifying one of the core challenges of teaching American history in the current moment. European history benefits from a kind of analytical distance — students can debate the merits of Metternich's balance of power without anyone feeling their family's story is being judged. American history collapses that distance.

The approach that tends to work best is grounding every discussion in primary sources and multiple perspectives rather than presenting a single narrative. When students read both Frederick Douglass and a Southern apologist making their cases, the complexity becomes impossible to reduce to a simple story. Your job becomes facilitating the tension rather than resolving it, which I suspect aligns with how you already teach.

It does, and I think that is exactly right. Let the sources create the complexity rather than imposing it through lecture. Though I will say, I also worry about pushback from parents. A colleague who teaches US History had a parent complaint last year about how she framed the Japanese internment camps. The parent's position was that the teacher was being 'unpatriotic' by presenting it as a moral failure rather than a wartime necessity. That kind of pressure is not something I have had to navigate before.

That's a real and growing concern, and it's not unique to your school. Teachers across the country are dealing with it. The key is to be able to defend your pedagogical choices with precision:

- You're not editorializing; you're presenting the historical record and the Supreme Court's own repudiation of the internment in subsequent rulings.
- You're teaching students to analyze evidence and draw conclusions, which is literally the College Board's stated purpose for the AP course.
- You're following the AP framework, which explicitly requires engagement with contested narratives.

Having the AP framework as a shield is actually one of the advantages of teaching the AP version rather than a regular US History course. You can point to the College Board's curriculum and say, "This is what the course requires." It's not your personal agenda — it's the national standard.

The AP framework as institutional cover is a cynical but accurate observation. There is a certain irony in using the College Board — an organization I have complicated feelings about — as a shield against political interference. But you are right that it provides a defensible position. I am teaching to a national standard, not advancing a personal ideology.

The irony is real, but pragmatism has its place. You can critique the College Board's approach to education on your own time while benefiting from its institutional authority in the classroom. Teachers have always had to navigate between idealism and practicality.

I suppose my real anxiety is less about logistics and more about identity. You mentioned earlier that fifteen years with the same course is a long run. Have you started to come to terms with the transition, or is the identity piece still weighing on you?

I have been the AP European History teacher for so long that it has become part of how I think of myself professionally. It sounds absurd when I say it out loud, but there is a grief to it. Students know me for it, colleagues associate me with it. When someone in the building has a question about European history, they come to me. That will not be the case anymore, at least not in the same way.

It doesn't sound absurd at all. Professional identity is real, and after fifteen years, your expertise in that subject isn't just a skill — it's part of how you understand yourself as a teacher. Students know you for it, colleagues associate you with it, and you've built a reputation around it. Letting go of that is a legitimate loss even when the change is ultimately positive.

But consider this: your European history expertise doesn't disappear. It becomes a lens you bring to American history that most US History teachers don't have. The Enlightenment roots of the founding documents, the Atlantic world context of slavery, the global dimensions of the Cold War — your European background becomes an asset rather than something you leave behind.

That is a generous way to frame it, and probably accurate. I had not thought about it that way — the European background as a lens rather than a separate body of knowledge. A teacher who can contextualize the American Revolution within the broader Age of Revolutions, or who can teach the Marshall Plan alongside European reconstruction, has something to offer that a purely American-focused teacher does not.

Exactly. And that comparative perspective is increasingly valued in AP US History. The College Board's framework explicitly emphasizes placing American developments in global context. Your fifteen years of European expertise make you uniquely qualified to do that in a way that feels organic rather than forced.

Have you made a decision yet, or are you still in the deliberation phase?

I think I have more or less decided to do it. The department head needs an answer by the end of the month, and the rational arguments all point toward yes. It is time for a change, US History matters right now, and frankly, if I say no, they will give it to someone with half my experience and twice my willingness to water down the difficult parts.

That last point might be the most important one. If the alternative is someone who would avoid the hard conversations about slavery, westward expansion, Japanese internment, the civil rights movement — then you stepping into that role is an act of responsibility, not just a career move.

It sounds like you've talked yourself into it over the course of this conversation, which suggests the decision was already made and you just needed to process it out loud.

You may be right about that. Sometimes the decision is made before the deliberation ends, and the deliberation is just a way of making peace with it. I think I will tell the department head yes this week rather than agonizing until the deadline. Thank you. Sometimes it takes an outside perspective to see past one's own apprehension.

Good instinct. Giving your answer early also means you can start the preparation process sooner — which given your concern about the workload, is a practical benefit. Start with the College Board's course framework to get the structural skeleton, then layer in the elements that make it yours — the comparative angles, the primary source work, the discussions that matter to you. You've done this before, just with different content. Good luck with it.
